nativeRange.habitat: Large whitewater rivers, blackwater tributaries, floodplain lakes (várzeas), and seasonally flooded forests; migrates in large shoals between whitewater spawning rivers and blackwater/clearwater feeding habitats with seasonal flood cycles

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rounded cobble for aufwuchs grazing surfaces
driftwood tangles mimicking submerged forest debris
robust or artificial plants only — soft-leaved plants will be consumed
strong directional flow (powerheads or wavemakers) — species requires current
subdued or dappled lighting to encourage biofilm growth on hardscape

diet.notes: Specialised aufwuchs grazer — uses its sucker-like protrusible mouth to scrape biofilm, algae, and organic detritus (periphyton) from hard surfaces. In captivity, offer algae wafers, spirulina discs, blanched spinach, blanched zucchini, lettuce, and cucumber daily; supplement with high-quality sinking pellets with vegetable content. Also accepts daphnia and frozen bloodworms as occasional protein supplement. Does not graze soft aquatic plants — it uproots and consumes them entirely. Constant grazing behaviour demands ad-lib algae growth on tank surfaces or regular supplemental feeding across the day.

goodWith: other large characins (e.g. silver dollars, pacus)
avoidWith: conspecifics in groups fewer than 6 — severe chasing and fin damage
small or nano fish easily harassed or eaten
soft-leaved aquatic plants — will be consumed
slow or sedate bottom-dwellers outcompeted at feeding
dwarf cichlids and other territory holders that cannot defend themselves

imageCandidates.notes: Side-profile aquarium specimen showing characteristic flag-patterned caudal fin. Photographer: B. Gratwicke.
Aquarium specimen photographed in the US by Frank M Greco. CC-BY licence — freely usable with attribution. Recommended pick.
Second aquarium-specimen shot by Frank M Greco (CC-BY). Good dorsal-fin and body-pattern detail.

summary: A large, active Amazonian characiform reaching 35 cm, prized for its striking red-and-yellow flag-patterned tail. A specialised aufwuchs grazer that requires very strong flow, a shoal of six or more, and a tank of 800 L minimum. Migratory spawning behaviour makes captive breeding impossible.
careNotes:
The Kissing Prochilodus is a shoaling, mid-water characiform from the central and western Amazon basin, where it undertakes long seasonal migrations between whitewater spawning rivers and blackwater feeding habitats in flooded forests. Its common trade name alludes to the protrusible, sucker-like mouth used to rasp periphyton and biofilm from hard substrates — a behaviour described as constant grazing. In the aquarium this translates to a demand for abundant grazing surfaces (smooth boulders, driftwood, tank glass) supplemented with daily vegetable matter; prepared algae wafers, spirulina-based sinking discs, and blanched greens should be offered multiple times per day.

Adult specimens reach approximately 35 cm TL and are powerfully built, requiring a tank of at least 800 L with a footprint no shorter than 200 cm to accommodate the species' active, longitudinal swimming style. Strong, directional flow is essential — powerheads or wavemakers should produce a current the fish must actively swim against. Without adequate current the fish becomes lethargic, develops fin issues, and deteriorates rapidly.

Conspecific dynamics are complex: in groups of five or fewer, dominant individuals inflict severe fin damage and chasing on subordinates. A minimum shoal of six is strongly recommended to distribute aggression; alternatively, a solitary specimen in a large community with other robust mid-to-large fish is workable. Suitable companions include large Loricariids, Doradid catfish, peaceful large cichlids such as Geophagus or Severum, and other large characins. Small fish will be harassed or eaten. Soft-leaved aquarium plants will be destroyed and eaten; only robust epiphytes on driftwood (Anubias, Java fern) attached out of reach, or artificial planting, should be used.

Water chemistry should lean slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.2) with low hardness (1–15°dH) and warm temperatures (23–29°C), reflecting Amazonian blackwater to whitewater conditions. The species is commercially harvested throughout Amazonia under the local name Jaraqui and is economically important as a food fish in Brazil. Its conservation status is Least Concern (IUCN 2020).

Note: despite the SeriouslyFish minimum of 540 L, the 800 L figure here reflects the hobbyistAdvisory constraint and accounts for proper shoaling numbers (six fish at 35 cm each).
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has never been recorded. In the wild, Semaprochilodus insignis is potamodromous — undertaking large-scale, seasonally triggered migrations in dense shoals from blackwater and clearwater feeding habitats into whitewater spawning rivers, driven by rising flood levels. This amphidromous-style migration involves hundreds of kilometres and specific hydrological triggers (temperature, turbidity, dissolved oxygen shifts, and photoperiod) that cannot be replicated in captivity. No hormone-induction protocol for this species has been published in the ornamental literature. Broodstock collection and pond spawning trials are conducted in Brazilian aquaculture research for restocking purposes, but these are outside the scope of the home hobby. Aquarists should purchase tank-raised or sustainably wild-collected specimens and not expect reproduction.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Semaprochilodus insignis
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided adult size (35 cm / 14 in), temperature (23–29°C), pH (5.5–7.2), hardness (1–15°H), minimum tank (540 L / 150×60×60 cm), diet (vegetable matter, algae wafers, frozen supplements), conspecific aggression dynamics (groups of 6+ or singly), compatibility list, and breeding status (unrecorded in hobby). Tank minimum raised to 800 L in this entry to reflect shoaling group of six at specialist-only advisory level.

additional.name: FishBase — Semaprochilodus insignis (ID 25658)
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Prochilodontidae, Characiformes), adult size (29.5 cm SL female — consistent with ~35 cm TL), temperature (22–26°C), pH (5.5–7.2), hardness (up to 15 dH), native range (central and western Amazon basin), potamodromous migration type, and IUCN Least Concern status. Also provided CC-BY image candidates from Frank M Greco. Note: FishBase temperature 22–26°C is slightly narrower than SeriouslyFish 23–29°C; used SeriouslyFish range as the aquarium-context source.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Semaprochilodus taeniurus (related species cross-reference)
additional.notes: Used as corroborating reference for congener husbandry (S. taeniurus: 30 cm, same temperature/pH/hardness/tank requirements). Confirms 540 L minimum tank, same diet, same conspecific aggression pattern, and breeding unrecorded in both species. Data consistent with S. insignis profile.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Semaprochilodus insignis
additional.notes: Confirmed migratory spawning behaviour (whitewater rivers for spawning, blackwater/clearwater feeding habitats), commercial fishery importance (Jaraqui), family Prochilodontidae classification, original description by Jardine 1841, and yellow fin colouration distinguishing S. insignis from red-finned congeners. Also confirmed aquarium trade presence with large-tank requirement.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding unrecorded. Wild spawning requires large-scale potamodromous migration into whitewater rivers triggered by seasonal flood conditions — impossible to replicate in aquaria. No hormone-induction data available for the ornamental hobby.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 35cm; active shoaling characiform needs 800L+ tank with strong flow. Migratory amphidromous breeding (impossible in captivity). Specialist large-fish setup.
